content:
When each player is instructed to resolve an effect, each player resolves it one at a time. If order is not specified, the first player decides.

If a player cannot fully resolve the effect, they resolve it as fully as possible.
See: Alteration Effect.
See: Ability, Cost.
See: Encounter Deck, Player Deck.

There are eight encounter card types: attachment, environment, minion, main scheme, obligation, side scheme, treachery, and villain cards. Encounter cards belong to various classifications (scenario-specific, modular set, etc.).

Most encounter cards have an orange card back.

• The encounter deck contains the encounter cards (attachments, environments, minions, side schemes, treacheries) players face during a scenario.
  • The order cannot be changed except by instruction.
  • If the encounter deck is empty, the encounter discard pile is immediately shuffled to create a new deck, and an acceleration token is placed next to the main scheme deck.
  • If a discard effect empties the encounter deck, the ability is considered fulfilled and does not continue into the newly shuffled deck.
  • If it empties during any other resolving game effect, that effect finishes after the deck is reset.
  • If both the encounter deck and discard pile are simultaneously empty, an infinite loop of acceleration tokens occurs and the players lose.
See: Discard Pile.

An encounter set is a grouping of encounter cards. Four types: scenario-specific, modular, the Standard set, and the Expert set.

Sets with the same name but different set icons are distinct sets.

To end the player phase:

  1. In player order, each may discard, then must discard to hand size.
  2. Each simultaneously draws to hand size.
  3. Each simultaneously readies all cards; ready exhausted encounter cards.
  4. Effects lasting 'until the end of the player phase' end.
  5. Resolve 'when/after the player phase ends' effects.
An enemy is a minion or villain. As a descriptor, 'enemy' refers to game elements belonging to the scenario ('enemy cards', 'enemy abilities', etc.).
See: Activation, Attack (Enemy Activation), Scheme (Enemy Activation).
See: Activation, Attack (Enemy Activation).
See: Activation, Scheme (Enemy Activation).
An energy resource is one of the four types of resources. Energy resources can be spent to pay resource costs of cards and abilities; some abilities specifically require energy resources.

When a minion enters play in a player's play area, it engages that player, unless otherwise specified. An engaged minion remains engaged with that player until defeated, removed from play, or an ability causes it to engage another player.

While engaged, a card ability cannot cause the minion to engage the same player again.
The phrase 'enters play' refers to any time a card transitions from an out-of-play area into play — via playing, putting into play by ability, or revealing from the encounter deck.

Environment is an encounter card type creating an overarching rule or set of rules for the scenario. An environment card enters play in the villain's play area and stays active while in play, until a card ability or game effect causes it to leave.

Event is a player card type generally played for an instantaneous effect.

  • When played, it's placed faceup on the table (not in play), costs paid, effects resolved (unless canceled), then placed in the owner's discard pile.
  • If an event has multiple triggered abilities, the player chooses one to trigger. Events are considered an extension of an identity — attacks/thwarts/defenses/actions/triggered abilities from playing an event are performed by that identity.
  • If an effect modifies damage/threat an event deals/removes multiple times, each instance is modified (except when modifying 'an attack' rather than 'an event' for multiple attacks — only the first is modified).
Excess damage is any amount of damage dealt to a character beyond that character's remaining hit points.

If a card is exhausted, it is rotated 90 degrees. An exhausted card cannot be exhausted again until ready.

A card ability on an exhausted card is still active, but if it must exhaust to pay a cost, that ability cannot be used until ready.
See: Modes of Play.

The expert set is added to scenarios during expert mode. It is not a modular encounter set and cannot be selected when a scenario requires choosing a modular set.

Cards in the 'Expert' classification have 'Expert' printed at the bottom of the card.
